questions.question:
Consider the following grammar G:
S -> F | H
F -> p | c
H -> d | c
Where S, F, and H are non-terminal symbols, p, d, and c are terminal symbols. Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?
S1: LL(1) can parse all strings that are generated using grammar G
S2: LR(1) can parse all strings that are generated using grammar G
A. Only S1
B. Only S2
C. Both S1 and S2
D. Neither S1 nor S2
questions.to_find: Whether LL(1) and/or LR(1) can parse all strings generated by grammar G

understand.plain: We have a grammar where S can derive F or H, and both F and H can derive the terminal 'c'. This creates a conflict because when we see 'c', we cannot decide whether to use S->F->c or S->H->c. We need to check if LL(1) (top-down with 1 lookahead) and LR(1) (bottom-up with 1 lookahead) can handle this situation.

keywords.explain: LL(1) parsing fails when the FIRST sets of two alternatives for the same non-terminal overlap. The parser cannot decide which production to use with just 1 lookahead token.
keywords.example: If A -> alpha | beta and FIRST(alpha) intersects FIRST(beta), LL(1) has a conflict.

keywords.explain: LR(1) fails when two different productions can reduce the same string with the same lookahead. Even with 1 lookahead, it cannot distinguish which rule to apply.
keywords.example: If both F -> c and H -> c can be reduced at end-of-input, LR(1) has a reduce-reduce conflict.

keywords.explain: A grammar is ambiguous if some string has two or more distinct parse trees. No deterministic parser (LL or LR of any fixed k) can parse an ambiguous grammar.
keywords.example: The string 'c' in grammar G can be derived as S->F->c or S->H->c, giving two parse trees.

260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S</text><line x1='90' y1='108' x2='90' y2='138' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='90' cy='150' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>F</text><line x1='90' y1='168' x2='90' y2='198' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='90' cy='210' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='90' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>c</text><circle cx='310' cy='90' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>S</text><line x1='310' y1='108' x2='310' y2='138' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='310' cy='150' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>H</text><line x1='310' y1='168' x2='310' y2='198' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='310' cy='210' r='18' class='no'/><text x='310' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>c</text><text x='200' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Both trees derive same string 'c' => Ambiguous!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two parse trees for the string 'c': left tree goes S->F->c, right tree goes S->H->c, showing the grammar is ambiguous.

given.aim: Determine whether LL(1) and LR(1) parsers can handle grammar G without conflicts.

terms.meaning: The set of terminals that can begin strings derived from F
terms.example: FIRST(F) = {p, c} since F -> p | c
terms.connects: Used to build the LL(1) parse table and check for conflicts.

terms.meaning: The set of terminals that can begin strings derived from H
terms.example: FIRST(H) = {d, c} since H -> d | c
terms.connects: FIRST(F) intersect FIRST(H) = {c} != empty, so LL(1) conflict exists.

terms.meaning: Claim that LL(1) can parse all strings of G
terms.example: For input 'c', LL(1) sees token 'c' and must choose S->F or S->H — conflict!
terms.connects: S1 is FALSE because FIRST(F) and FIRST(H) share the terminal 'c'.

terms.meaning: Claim that LR(1) can parse all strings of G
terms.example: After shifting 'c' with lookahead '$', LR(1) must choose F->c or H->c — reduce-reduce conflict!
terms.connects: S2 is FALSE because the grammar is ambiguous; no deterministic parser can handle it.

given.plan: Check LL(1) by computing FIRST sets and verifying no overlap for S's alternatives. Check LR(1) by noting that the grammar is inherently ambiguous (two parse trees for 'c'), making any deterministic parser fail.

steps.title: Check LL(1) feasibility for grammar G
steps.formula_raw: LL(1) condition: FIRST(alpha) intersect FIRST(beta) = empty for A -> alpha | beta
steps.apply: S -> F | H. FIRST(F) = {p, c}, FIRST(H) = {d, c}. FIRST(F) intersect FIRST(H) = {c} != empty. When the parser sees input 'c', it cannot decide whether to expand S -> F or S -> H using only 1 lookahead. Therefore S1 is FALSE — LL(1) fails.
steps.note: The terminal 'c' appears in productions of both F and H, which are both alternatives of S, causing an LL(1) conflict.

steps.title: Check LR(1) feasibility for grammar G
steps.formula_raw: LR(1) condition: no shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts in LR(1) item sets
steps.apply: After shifting 'c' onto the stack, LR(1) must decide whether to reduce using F -> c or H -> c. Both F and H are derived from S with no further context. The lookahead in both cases is $ (end of input). This is a reduce-reduce conflict: [F -> c ., $] and [H -> c ., $] coexist in the same LR(1) state. The grammar is effectively ambiguous for the string 'c'. Therefore S2 is FALSE — LR(1) also fails.
steps.note: The grammar is ambiguous because the string 'c' has two distinct parse trees (S->F->c and S->H->c). No deterministic parser of any fixed lookahead k can parse an ambiguous grammar.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: S1 is FALSE (LL(1) has a conflict at terminal 'c'). S2 is FALSE (LR(1) has a reduce-reduce conflict at terminal 'c'). Hence neither S1 nor S2 is correct.
steps.note: The root cause is the grammar's inherent ambiguity — the same terminal 'c' can be derived via two different non-terminals (F and H), both reachable from S.

solution.result: D. Neither S1 nor S2 — both LL(1) and LR(1) fail to parse grammar G because of conflicts caused by the ambiguous derivation of terminal 'c'.

questions.formula_note: LL(1) conflict condition: FIRST sets of alternatives must be disjoint. LR(1) reduce-reduce conflict: ambiguous grammars always cause conflicts in any deterministic parser.

questions.question:
Consider the following grammar:
stmt -> if expr then expr else expr; stmt | empty
expr -> term relop term | term
term -> id | number
id -> a | b | c
number -> 0 | 1 | ... | 9
where relop is a relational operator (e.g., <, >, <=), empty refers to the empty statement, and if, then, else are terminals.
Consider a program P following the above grammar containing ten if terminals. The number of control flow paths in P is ___.
For example, the program:
  if c1 then c2 else c3
has 2 control flow paths: c1 -> c2 and c1 -> c3.
A. 2^10
B. 10
C. 2^10 + 1
D. 2
questions.to_find: Number of control flow paths in a program P containing ten if terminals

understand.plain: Each 'if-then-else' statement creates a branch: execution goes either to the 'then' branch or the 'else' branch. With 10 independent if statements, each one doubles the number of possible paths through the program. So with 10 if statements, the total number of control flow paths is 2^10 = 1024.

keywords.explain: A control flow path is a sequence of statements that can be executed from program start to end, following one branch at each decision point.
keywords.example: In 'if c1 then c2 else c3', there are 2 paths: (c1 true -> c2) and (c1 false -> c3).

keywords.explain: Each if-then-else construct introduces exactly one binary decision point, multiplying the number of paths by 2.
keywords.example: 1 if-else: 2 paths. 2 nested if-else: 4 paths. n if-else: 2^n paths.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='220' viewBox='0 0 420 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Control Flow Paths with n if-then-else</text><rect x='170' y='45' width='80' height='30' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>START</text><line x1='210' y1='75' x2='210' y2='95' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='205,95 215,95 210,105' class='hi'/><rect x='155' y='105' width='110' height='30' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>if_1 (branch)</text><line x1='155' y1='120' x2='80' y2='150' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='265' y1='120' x2='340' y2='150' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='145' class='sub ok'>true</text><text x='300' y='145' class='sub no'>false</text><rect x='30' y='155' width='100' height='28' rx='5' class='ok'/><text x='80' y='174' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>then-branch</text><rect x='290' y='155' width='100' height='28' rx='5' class='no'/><text x='340' y='174' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>else-branch</text><text x='210' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Each if doubles paths: n if-else => 2^n paths total</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A binary decision tree: START node leads to if_1 branch, which splits into true (then-branch) and false (else-branch). Each additional if doubles the number of leaf paths.

given.aim: Count the total number of distinct control flow paths through program P which contains exactly 10 if terminals.

terms.meaning: P contains exactly 10 if-then-else constructs according to the given grammar
terms.example: P could be structured as nested or sequential if-then-else statements
terms.connects: Each if contributes one binary decision point, determining the number of paths.

terms.meaning: Each if-then-else has exactly 2 outcomes: then-branch (condition true) or else-branch (condition false)
terms.example: if c1 then c2 else c3 -> 2 paths
terms.connects: With 10 such decisions, total paths = 2 * 2 * ... (10 times) = 2^10.
This is the total number of control flow paths through a program with 10 independent if-then-else branches.

given.plan: Recognize that each if-then-else independently doubles the number of control flow paths. With 10 if terminals, the number of paths is 2^10.

steps.title: Establish the base case
steps.apply: A program with zero if-then-else constructs has exactly 1 control flow path (just sequential execution). The example given: 'if c1 then c2 else c3' has 2 paths.
steps.note: Each if-then-else adds exactly one binary branch point.

steps.title: Count paths for n if-then-else statements
steps.apply: Each additional if-then-else multiplies the path count by 2. For 10 if terminals: paths = 2^10 = 1024. Whether the if statements are nested or sequential, each introduces one binary choice that doubles the total paths.
steps.note: The grammar rule 'stmt -> if expr then expr else expr; stmt' allows sequential composition, and nesting within expressions. In all configurations, 10 if constructs yield 2^10 paths.

solution.result: A. 2^10 — A program with 10 if-then-else constructs has 2^10 = 1024 control flow paths.

questions.formula_note: Each if-then-else binary branch doubles the control flow paths: n if-else statements yield 2^n distinct execution paths.

questions.question:
Consider the following parse tree for the expression a$b$c$d#e#f, involving two binary operators $ and #.

[Parse tree: The root is #, with left subtree rooted at # containing e and f, and the left child of that # is another # node. The leftmost subtree rooted at $ contains a$b$c$d where $ groups left to right.]

Actually, from the parse tree shown: the # operator groups as a#(b#(c)) style at top level, and $ groups as ((a$b)$c)$d at the lower level, meaning $ has higher precedence (appears deeper in tree) and is left associative; # is right associative (groups right to left).

Which one of the following is correct for the given parse tree?
A. $ has higher precedence and is left associative; # is right associative
B. # has higher precedence and is left associative; $ is right associative
C. $ has higher precedence and is right associative; # is left associative
D. $ has higher precedence and is left associative; # is left associative
questions.answer: A. $ has higher precedence and is left associative; # is right associative
questions.to_find: The precedence and associativity of operators $ and # based on the given parse tree

understand.plain: In a parse tree, higher precedence operators appear deeper (closer to leaves) because they bind more tightly. Associativity is determined by how a sequence of the same operator groups: left-associative means it groups left-to-right (left subtree is recursive), right-associative means it groups right-to-left (right subtree is recursive). We read these properties directly from the parse tree structure.

keywords.explain: Higher precedence operators appear at deeper levels in the parse tree because they bind operands before lower precedence operators.
keywords.example: In a+b*c, the * node is deeper than the + node, showing * has higher precedence.

keywords.explain: A left-associative operator groups operands from left to right. In a parse tree, the left child of the operator node is another occurrence of the same operator (left-recursive tree).
keywords.example: a$b$c parsed as (a$b)$c: the root $ has left child (a$b) and right child c.

keywords.explain: A right-associative operator groups operands from right to left. In a parse tree, the right child of the operator node is another occurrence of the same operator (right-recursive tree).
keywords.example: a#b#c parsed as a#(b#c): the root # has left child a and right child (b#c).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='300' viewBox='0 0 480 300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe x2='340' y2='95' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='140' cy='110' r='18' class='no'/><text x='140' y='116' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>#</text><line x1='122' y1='120' x2='70' y2='150' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='158' y1='120' x2='200' y2='150' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='70' cy='165' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='70' y='171' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink' style='fill:white'>$</text><line x1='52' y1='175' x2='30' y2='205' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='88' y1='175' x2='110' y2='205' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='30' cy='218' r='14' class='ok'/><text x='30' y='224' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>$</text><line x1='20' y1='228' x2='10' y2='255' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='228' x2='50' y2='255' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='10' y='268' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a$b</text><text x='50' y='268' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>c</text><text x='110' y='218' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>d</text><text x='200' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e</text><text x='340' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>f</text><text x='240' y='285' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>$ deeper(higher prec, left-assoc); # at top(lower prec, right-assoc)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree with # at root (right-recursive: left child is another # node), and $ deeper in the tree (left-recursive: left child is another $ node). $ has higher precedence and is left associative; # is right associative.

given.aim: Read operator precedence and associativity from the parse tree structure of a$b$c$d#e#f.

terms.meaning: A string involving 3 uses of $ and 2 uses of # as binary operators over 6 operands
terms.example: a,b,c,d are operands of $; e,f are operands of #
terms.connects: The parse tree structure reveals how these operators bind their operands.

terms.term: Depth of operator in parse tree
terms.meaning: An operator that appears at deeper tree levels has higher precedence (binds tighter)
terms.example: $ nodes are deeper than # nodes in the given parse tree

terms.meaning: Left-recursive: left child is same operator (left-associative). Right-recursive: right child is same operator (right-associative).
terms.example: ((a$b)$c)$d is left-recursive for $; a#(b#(c)) is right-recursive for #
terms.connects: From the parse tree: $ groups left-to-right, # groups right-to-left.

given.plan: Read the parse tree from the image: identify which operator is at the root (lower precedence) vs deeper (higher precedence), then check which subtree is recursive for each operator to determine associativity.

steps.title: Determine operator precedence from parse tree depth
steps.formula_raw: Higher precedence operator = appears at deeper level in parse tree
steps.apply: In the given parse tree for a$b$c$d#e#f, the root node is #. The $ operator nodes appear at deeper levels within the left subtree of #. Since # is at the top (shallower) and $ is deeper, $ has HIGHER precedence than #. This means $ binds its operands more tightly — consistent with the expression being parsed as (((a$b)$c)$d) # e # f.
steps.note: Higher precedence => appears lower (deeper) in parse tree, not at the root.

steps.title: Determine associativity of $ from parse tree structure
steps.formula_raw: Left-associative: left child = same operator (left-skewed subtree)
steps.apply: Looking at the $ subtree: the parse tree for a$b$c$d is left-skewed — the left child of the topmost $ is another $ node (containing a$b$c), and the right child is d. This is a left-recursive structure, meaning $ is LEFT ASSOCIATIVE. Equivalently: a$b$c$d is parsed as ((a$b)$c)$d.
steps.note: Left-skewed tree (left child is recursive) = left-associative operator.

steps.title: Determine associativity of # from parse tree structure
steps.formula_raw: Right-associative: right child = same operator (right-skewed subtree)
steps.apply: Looking at the # subtree: the root # has a left child (the $ subtree containing a$b$c$d) and a right child that is another # node (containing e and f). This is a right-recursive structure, meaning # is RIGHT ASSOCIATIVE. Equivalently: x#e#f (where x = a$b$c$d) is parsed as x#(e#f).
steps.note: Right-skewed tree (right child is recursive) = right-associative operator.

steps.title: Match with given options
steps.apply: Conclusion: $ has higher precedence (deeper in tree) and is left associative (left-skewed). # has lower precedence (at root) and is right associative (right-skewed). This matches Option A.
steps.note: This is the standard technique: precedence from depth, associativity from which side is recursive.

solution.result: A. $ has higher precedence and is left associative; # is right associative.

questions.formula_note: Operator precedence is read from parse tree depth (deeper = higher precedence); associativity is read from tree skew direction (left-skewed subtree = left-associative, right-skewed = right-associative).
